title: Lower Styria

infobox.value: ([/ˈstɪəriə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *Štajerska* [[ˈʃtáːjɛɾska]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene")), also known as **Slovenian Styria** (*Slovenska Štajerska*; [Austrian German](/wiki/Austrian_German_language "Austrian German language"): *Slowenische Steiermark*[[1]](#cite_note-1)) or **Lower Styria** (*Spodnja Štajerska*; *Untersteiermark*) to differentiate it from [Austrian Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), is a traditional region in northeastern [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), comprising the southern third of the former [Duchy of Styria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Styria "Duchy of Styria"). The population of Styria in its historical boundaries amounts to around 705,000 inhabitants, or 34.5% of the population of Slovenia. Its largest city and urban center is [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), with other urban centers including [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"), [Velenje](/wiki/Velenje "Velenje"), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj") and [Trbovlje](/wiki/Trbovlje "Trbovlje").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Lower Styria](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Styria&redirect=no "Lower Styria"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Borders_of_the_Historical_Habsburgian_Lands_in_the_Republic_of_Slovenia.png/330px-Borders_of_the_Historical_Habsburgian_Lands_in_the_Republic_of_Slovenia.png)](/wiki/File:Borders_of_the_Historical_Habsburgian_Lands_in_the_Republic_of_Slovenia.png)

2. [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola"): 2a [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Carniola "Upper Carniola"), 2b [Inner](/wiki/Inner_Carniola "Inner Carniola"), 2c [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Carniola "Lower Carniola")
3. [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(Slovenia) "Carinthia (Slovenia)")

**Styria** ([/ˈstɪəriə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *Štajerska* [[ˈʃtáːjɛɾska]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene")), also known as **Slovenian Styria** (*Slovenska Štajerska*; [Austrian German](/wiki/Austrian_German_language "Austrian German language"): *Slowenische Steiermark*[[1]](#cite_note-1)) or **Lower Styria** (*Spodnja Štajerska*; *Untersteiermark*) to differentiate it from [Austrian Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), is a traditional region in northeastern [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), comprising the southern third of the former [Duchy of Styria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Styria "Duchy of Styria"). The population of Styria in its historical boundaries amounts to around 705,000 inhabitants, or 34.5% of the population of Slovenia. Its largest city and urban center is [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), with other urban centers including [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"), [Velenje](/wiki/Velenje "Velenje"), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj") and [Trbovlje](/wiki/Trbovlje "Trbovlje").

## Use of the term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Styria_(Slovenia)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Use of the term")]

In the 19th century, the Styrian duchy, which existed as a distinct political-administrative entity from 1056 to 1918, used to be divided into three traditional regions: [Upper Styria](/wiki/Upper_Styria "Upper Styria") (*Obersteiermark*; *Zgornja Štajerska*), Central Styria (*Mittelsteiermark*; *Srednja Štajerska*), and Lower Styria, stretching from the [Mur](/wiki/Mur_(river) "Mur (river)") River and the [Slovene Hills](/wiki/Slovene_Hills "Slovene Hills") in the north down to the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava"). Upper Styria and Central Styria, predominantly German-speaking, today form the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") state of [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria") (*Steiermark*). The southern third, predominantly Slovene-speaking Lower Styria, became part of the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") (Yugoslavia) after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it became part of the predecessor of modern [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), the [Socialist Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia").

Although used interchangeably at times, the term "Southern Styria" (*Südsteiermark*) refers to the southern districts of the Austrian state of Styria, whereas the term "Lower Styria" (*Spodnja Štajerska*) refers to the region of Styria in northeastern Slovenia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Slovene name *Štajerska* is borrowed and adapted from the German name for the region, *Steiermark*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Both ultimately derive from [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages"), via the river [Steyr](/wiki/Steyr_(river) "Steyr (river)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Styria_(Slovenia)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the Lower Styrian lands were ruled by several [immediate](/wiki/Imperial_immediacy "Imperial immediacy") (*reichsfrei*) dynasties like the [Counts of Celje](/wiki/Counts_of_Celje "Counts of Celje"), whose large possessions were not incorporated by the [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") dukes until the 15th century.

According to the last [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") census of 1910, Lower Styria had around 498,000 inhabitants, of which around 82% were Slovene and around 18% German speakers.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 1918, after the disintegration of the Austro-Hungarian monarchy following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the Duchy of Styria was divided between the newly established states of [German Austria](/wiki/German_Austria "German Austria") and the Yugoslav [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs"). In early November 1918, [Rudolf Maister](/wiki/Rudolf_Maister "Rudolf Maister"), a Slovene major of the former [Austro-Hungarian Army](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Army "Austro-Hungarian Army"), with about 4,000 local volunteers occupied Lower Styria and the town of Maribor and claimed it for Yugoslavia. After a short fight with German-Austrian paramilitary units, the current border was established, acknowledged by the provisional Styrian assembly at [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz"). By December 1918, all of Lower Styria was *de facto* included in the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). A protest by German-speaking [Marburg](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor") citizens resulted in the [Marburg Bloody Sunday](/wiki/Marburg_Bloody_Sunday "Marburg Bloody Sunday"), when 13 people were killed and about 60 wounded.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Confirmed by the 1919 [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)"), the border between Yugoslav and Austrian Styria mostly followed the ethnic-linguistic dividing line between [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") and [ethnic Germans](/wiki/Ethnic_Germans "Ethnic Germans"). Nevertheless, several predominantly German-speaking towns were annexed into Yugoslavia, such as [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor") (*Marburg an der Drau*) (80% German speakers), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj") (*Pettau*) (86%), and [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje") (*Cilli*) (67%); in addition, the German-speaking area around the village of [Apače](/wiki/Apa%C4%8De "Apače") (*Abstall*) was annexed to Yugoslavia. According to the 1921 Yugoslav census, some 22,500 ethnic Germans lived in Yugoslav Lower Styria. They represented around 4.5% of the overall population of the region, and around 57% of all ethnic Germans in Slovenia. In 1931, this number dropped to around 12,500 or 2.3% of the regional population, and around 45% of all ethnic Germans in Slovenia.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1922, the County of Maribor was formed, comprising most of the territory of Slovene Styria, plus the [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") and the [Međimurje](/wiki/Me%C4%91imurje "Međimurje") regions. After the *[coup d'etat](/wiki/Coup_d%27etat "Coup d'etat")* of [King Alexander I of Yugoslavia](/wiki/King_Alexander_I_of_Yugoslavia "King Alexander I of Yugoslavia") in January 1929, the counties were abolished and replaced with nine [Banates](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Subdivisions of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia") (Slovene: *Banovina*).[[7]](#cite_note-7) Following the reorganization implemented by the Yugoslav constitution of 1931, Slovene Styria was incorporated in the newly established [Drava Banovina](/wiki/Dravska_Banovina "Dravska Banovina"), which was more or less identical with Slovenia, with [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") as its capital city.

In April 1941, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") [invaded Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") and Slovene Styria was annexed, becoming *[CdZ](/wiki/Chief_of_Civil_Administration "Chief of Civil Administration")-Gebiet Untersteiermark* under *[Reichsgau Steiermark](/wiki/Reichsgau_Steiermark "Reichsgau Steiermark")*. A policy of violent Germanization was introduced. Public use of Slovene was prohibited, and all Slovene associations were dissolved. Members of all professional and intellectual groups, including many clergymen, were expelled. Between April 1941 and May 1942, around 80,000 Slovenes (almost 15% of the overall population) were expelled from Lower Styria, or resettled to other parts of the Reich. As a reaction, a [resistance movement](/wiki/Slovene_Partisans "Slovene Partisans") developed. Many areas of Lower Styria witnessed fierce fighting between German troops and Slovene partisan units.

After World War II, [Yugoslav](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") authority over the region was established and Slovene Styria became an integral part of the [Socialist Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia"). According to prior decisions made by the [Anti-Fascist Council of the People's Liberation of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Anti-Fascist_Council_of_the_People%27s_Liberation_of_Yugoslavia "Anti-Fascist Council of the People's Liberation of Yugoslavia"), an expulsion of the remaining ethnic German population was carried out, regardless of their links to the Nazi regime.

Between the 1950s and 1970s, many areas of the region underwent rapid industrialization. Towns like Maribor, Celje, and Velenje became among the most important industrial centers of Slovenia and Yugoslavia.

## Statistical division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Styria_(Slovenia)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Statistical division")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Slovenia%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_%28statistical_regions%29_-_colored.svg/250px-Slovenia%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_%28statistical_regions%29_-_colored.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Slovenia,_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_(statistical_regions)_-_colored.svg)

Statistical regions of Slovenia

Lower Styria has no official status as an administrative or statistical unit within Slovenia. In 2005, the country was divided into 12 statistical regions. The bulk of Lower Styria is subdivided between the [Drava Statistical Region](/wiki/Drava_Statistical_Region "Drava Statistical Region") ([Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *podravska statistična regija*) with its seat in [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), and the [Savinja Statistical Region](/wiki/Savinja_Statistical_Region "Savinja Statistical Region") (*savinjska statistična regija*) with its seat in [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"). Smaller areas of Lower Styria are included in:

* The [Mura Statistical Region](/wiki/Mura_Statistical_Region "Mura Statistical Region") (*pomurska statistična regija*): the subregion called [Prlekija](/wiki/Prlekija "Prlekija"), with the municipalities of [Apače](/wiki/Municipality_of_Apa%C4%8De "Municipality of Apače"), [Gornja Radgona](/wiki/Municipality_of_Gornja_Radgona "Municipality of Gornja Radgona"), [Križevci](/wiki/Municipality_of_Kri%C5%BEevci "Municipality of Križevci"), [Ljutomer](/wiki/Municipality_of_Ljutomer "Municipality of Ljutomer"), [Radenci](/wiki/Municipality_of_Radenci "Municipality of Radenci"), [Razkrižje](/wiki/Municipality_of_Razkri%C5%BEje "Municipality of Razkrižje"), [Sveti Jurij ob Ščavnici](/wiki/Municipality_of_Sveti_Jurij_ob_%C5%A0%C4%8Davnici "Municipality of Sveti Jurij ob Ščavnici"), and [Veržej](/wiki/Municipality_of_Ver%C5%BEej "Municipality of Veržej");
* The [Carinthia Statistical Region](/wiki/Carinthia_Statistical_Region "Carinthia Statistical Region") (*koroška statistična regija*): the municipalities of [Mislinja](/wiki/Municipality_of_Mislinja "Municipality of Mislinja"), [Muta](/wiki/Municipality_of_Muta "Municipality of Muta"), [Podvelka](/wiki/Municipality_of_Podvelka "Municipality of Podvelka"), [Radlje ob Dravi](/wiki/Municipality_of_Radlje_ob_Dravi "Municipality of Radlje ob Dravi"), [Ribnica na Pohorju](/wiki/Municipality_of_Ribnica_na_Pohorju "Municipality of Ribnica na Pohorju"), [Slovenj Gradec](/wiki/City_Municipality_of_Slovenj_Gradec "City Municipality of Slovenj Gradec"), and [Vuzenica](/wiki/Municipality_of_Vuzenica "Municipality of Vuzenica");
* The [Lower Sava Statistical Region](/wiki/Lower_Sava_Statistical_Region "Lower Sava Statistical Region") (*spodnjeposavska statistična regija*): the [Municipality of Bistrica ob Sotli](/wiki/Municipality_of_Bistrica_ob_Sotli "Municipality of Bistrica ob Sotli"), and the territory on the left bank of the [Sava River](/wiki/Sava "Sava") in the municipalities of [Brežice](/wiki/Municipality_of_Bre%C5%BEice "Municipality of Brežice"), [Krško](/wiki/Municipality_of_Kr%C5%A1ko "Municipality of Krško"), [Sevnica](/wiki/Municipality_of_Sevnica "Municipality of Sevnica"), and [Radeče](/wiki/Municipality_of_Rade%C4%8De "Municipality of Radeče");
* The [Central Sava Statistical Region](/wiki/Central_Sava_Statistical_Region "Central Sava Statistical Region") (*zasavska statistična regija*): the territory on the left bank of the Sava River in the municipalities of [Hrastnik](/wiki/Municipality_of_Hrastnik "Municipality of Hrastnik") and [Trbovlje](/wiki/Municipality_of_Trbovlje "Municipality of Trbovlje").

Nowadays, many of these peripheral areas are no longer considered part of Styria. An exception is the [Prlekija](/wiki/Prlekija "Prlekija") subregion, which is still widely considered part of the region. The name *Štajerska* disappeared from official use in 1922, when the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes was administratively divided into [oblasts](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia#Oblasts_(1922–1929) "Subdivisions of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). Nevertheless, the name is still very much alive in both colloquial and media language, and it is part of established cultural and geographical terminology. It also continues to be employed in the promotion of tourism.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Cities and towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Styria_(Slovenia)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cities and towns")]

The cultural and economic centre of Lower Styria has always been the city of [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"). Other major towns are [Brežice](/wiki/Bre%C5%BEice "Brežice"), [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj"), [Slovenj Gradec](/wiki/Slovenj_Gradec "Slovenj Gradec"), [Slovenska Bistrica](/wiki/Slovenska_Bistrica "Slovenska Bistrica"), and [Velenje](/wiki/Velenje "Velenje").

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Styria_(Slovenia)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Styria is known for its [white wine](/wiki/White_wine "White wine"), especially [Ljutomer Riesling](/w/index.php?title=Ljutomer_Riesling&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ljutomer Riesling (page does not exist)"), for the [Pohorje](/wiki/Pohorje "Pohorje") ski resort, for cultural festivals and for [pumpkin seed oil](/wiki/Pumpkin_seed_oil "Pumpkin seed oil"). It is also known as a hop-growing area producing [Styrian Goldings](/wiki/Styrian_Goldings "Styrian Goldings"), a variety of the English aroma hop Fuggles.
